spec_match.rationale: Registers an autonomous custom element with `attachInternals({ behaviors: [HTMLSubmitButtonBehavior] })` and falls back to `closest('form').requestSubmit()` when the behavior is missing â€” that is exactly the surface the spec defines.

interactive.rationale: Real form with two inputs, a real custom element submit, a live event log that timestamps registrations and submissions.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises a custom element that submits the surrounding form when activated; the page renders exactly that.

edge_cases.rationale: The form uses `novalidate`, which bypasses the validation half of the platform behaviour the feature provides. Disabled state, Enter-key vs click, and the validation-blocked-submit path are not demonstrated on this page (the sibling validation-relay covers some).

design_system.rationale: Variables-only and no static-card, but the `fancy-submit` element uses `cursor: pointer` plus a gradient background and doesn't have a visible `:focus-visible` outline â€” keyboard activation visibility is the headline use-case of the behavior and the styling currently hides it.

openQuestions.title: Drop `novalidate` and demonstrate validation-blocked submit
openQuestions.detail: The form currently sets `novalidate`, which means even though both inputs have `required` and `minlength`, the submission goes through without validation. The whole point of `HTMLSubmitButtonBehavior` over `requestSubmit()` is that the platform runs validation. Remove `novalidate`, then add a `try with empty input` button that demonstrates the platform refusing the submit and showing the constraint-violation UI.

openQuestions.title: Add a `:focus-visible` outline to fancy-submit
openQuestions.detail: The element is tab-focusable (`tabindex="0"`) but the page-local CSS shadow + transform on `:hover` does not include any focus styling. Keyboard activation parity is exactly what the behavior is for, so the keyboard path needs to be visually first-class. Add a high-contrast `:focus-visible` outline using `--accent-blue` plus a 2px offset.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate disabled state
openQuestions.detail: Spec says `HTMLSubmitButtonBehavior` wires up `:disabled` handling. Add a `disable / enable` toggle and a `:disabled` style; show that the form submission is blocked while disabled, mirroring native button semantics.

openQuestions.title: Add a side-by-side native button for comparison
openQuestions.detail: The blurb implies the element is `exactly as a native submit button would be`. Mount a `<button type="submit">` next to the fancy-submit so the visitor can hit Enter / Space / click on both and see they behave identically. The sibling `keyboard-activation` concept already does this â€” that pattern should live on the headline demo too.
